rows.entry_description: Data contamination occurs when incorrect data is used for training. For example, data that is not aligned with model's purpose or data that is already set aside for other development tasks such as testing and evaluation.
rows.register_names: Poor data quality and representativeness
rows.note: Incorrect or already-seen data used in training degrades data quality; the benchmark-leak angle is MR-046.

rows.entry_description: Overfitting occurs when a model or algorithm memorizes and fits too closely or exactly to its training data. Overfitting results in a model that might not be able to make accurate predictions or conclusions from any data other than the training data and potentially fails in unexpected scenarios. Overfitting is also related to model collapse, which involves repeatedly training generative models on synthetic data that is generated with LLMs causing the model to lose information and become less accurate.
rows.register_names: Lack of robustness to distribution shift and edge cases
rows.note: Overfitting causes generalization failure on shifted or edge inputs; the accuracy angle is MR-050.

rows.entry_description: Unrepresentative data occurs when the training or fine-tuning data is not sufficiently representative of the underlying population or does not measure the phenomenon of interest. Synthetic data might not fully capture the complexity and nuances of real-world data. Causes include possible limitations in the seed data quality, biases in generation methods, or inadequate domain knowledge. Thus, AI models might struggle to generalize effectively to real-world scenarios.
rows.register_names: Poor data quality and representativeness
rows.note: Training data not representative of the population is poor data quality and representativeness.

rows.entry_description: Laws and other regulations might limit the collection of certain types of data for specific AI use cases.
rows.register_names: Unlawful or non-consensual collection and processing of personal data
rows.note: Legal limits on collecting data are a lawful-processing constraint; the generic-compliance angle is MR-040.

rows.entry_description: Laws and other restrictions can limit or prohibit transferring data.
rows.register_names: Unlawful or non-consensual collection and processing of personal data
rows.note: Legal limits on transferring data are a lawful-processing constraint; the generic-compliance angle is MR-040.

rows.entry_description: Laws and other restrictions can limit or prohibit the use of some data for specific AI use cases.
rows.register_names: Unlawful or non-consensual collection and processing of personal data
rows.note: Legal limits on using data for a use case are a lawful-processing constraint; the generic-compliance angle is MR-040.

rows.entry_description: Historical and societal biases might be present in data that are used to train and fine-tune models. Biases can also be inherited from seed data or exacerbated by synthetic data generation methods.
rows.register_names: Biased or discriminatory outputs and decisions
rows.note: Historical and societal bias in training data is the canonical bias risk; the data-quality mechanism is MR-059.

rows.entry_description: Confidential information might be included as part of the data that is used to train or tune the model.
rows.register_names: Disclosure of confidential or proprietary information
rows.note: Confidential information in training data risks disclosure of confidential information.

rows.entry_description: Terms of service, license compliance, or other IP issues may restrict the ability to use certain data for building models.
rows.register_names: Intellectual property and copyright infringement
rows.note: Terms-of-service, licence or IP limits on training data are IP and copyright compliance.

rows.entry_description: Applicable laws can establish data subject rights such as opt-out rights, right to access, and right to be forgotten. Synthetic data might raise unique concerns, such as the potential for reidentification of individuals from seemingly anonymous synthetic data. Data subject rights might also be relevant in scenarios where synthetic data is derived from sensitive or personal information.
rows.register_names: Unlawful or non-consensual collection and processing of personal data
rows.note: Data-subject rights such as access, erasure and opt-out are lawful-processing obligations.

rows.entry_description: Inclusion or presence of personal identifiable information (PII) and sensitive personal information (SPI) in the data used for training or fine tuning the model might result in unwanted disclosure of that information.
rows.register_names: Unlawful or non-consensual collection and processing of personal data
rows.note: PII or SPI in training data is collection and processing of personal data.

rows.entry_description: Even with the removal of personal information (PI) and sensitive personal information (SPI) from data, it might be possible to identify persons due to correlations to other features available in the data.
rows.register_names: Privacy-invasive inference and re-identification
rows.note: Re-identifying individuals after PI removal is privacy-invasive inference and re-identification.

rows.entry_description: A type of adversarial attack where an adversary or malicious insider injects intentionally corrupted, false, misleading, or incorrect samples into the training or fine-tuning datasets.
rows.register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
rows.note: Injecting corrupted samples into training data is data and model poisoning and backdoors.

rows.entry_description: Proper documentation contains information about how a model's data was collected, curated, and used to train a model, including any synthetic data generation processes. Without proper documentation it might be harder to satisfactorily explain the behavior of the model.
rows.register_names: Insufficient documentation, transparency and data provenance
rows.note: Insufficient documentation of data collection and curation is the documentation and provenance risk.

rows.entry_description: Data provenance refers to the traceability of data (including synthetic data), which includes its ownership, origin, transformations, and generation. Proving that the data is the same as the original source with correct usage terms is difficult without standardized methods for verifying data sources or generation.
rows.register_names: Insufficient documentation, transparency and data provenance
rows.note: Untraceable data origin and transformations is the documentation and data-provenance risk.

rows.entry_description: Improper collection, generation, and preparation of training or tuning data can result in data label errors, conflicting information or misinformation.
rows.register_names: Poor data quality and representativeness
rows.note: Label errors and conflicting information from poor curation is poor data quality.

rows.entry_description: Using undesirable output (for example, inaccurate, inappropriate, and user content) for retraining purposes can result in unexpected model behavior.
rows.register_names: Model performance drift and degradation over time
rows.note: Retraining on undesirable output causes degradation and feedback-loop drift; the data-quality angle is MR-059.

rows.note: Eavesdropping / logging sensitive conversations = surveillance enablement; data-leakage angle MR-009.

rows.entry_description: Sensor Spoofing
rows.register_names: Adversarial examples and evasion attacks
rows.note: Sensor spoofing of cyber-physical systems = physical-world adversarial input; physical-safety impact MR-049.

rows.entry_description: Fraudulent Use
rows.register_names: AI-enabled fraud, scams and social engineering
rows.note: Fraud / spam / social-engineering generation.

rows.entry_name: Abuse of APIs for Mass Automation
rows.entry_description: Malicious Workflows
rows.register_names: Deliberate misuse and repurposing for harm
rows.note: Malicious workflows (mass automation, dedicated malicious infrastructure) = misuse/repurposing.

rows.entry_description: Malicious Workflows
rows.register_names: Deliberate misuse and repurposing for harm
rows.note: Malicious workflows (mass automation, dedicated malicious infrastructure) = misuse/repurposing.

rows.entry_description: Cross-Modal Inconsistency Exploits
rows.register_names: Prompt injection and jailbreaking
rows.note: Cross-modal inconsistency exploits = multi-modal injection / adversarial.

rows.entry_description: Cross-Modal Inconsistency Exploits
rows.register_names: Prompt injection and jailbreaking
rows.note: Cross-modal inconsistency exploits = multi-modal injection / adversarial.

rows.entry_description: Fusion Payload Split
rows.register_names: Prompt injection and jailbreaking
rows.note: Fusion payload split = obfuscated multi-modal injection.

rows.entry_description: Fusion Payload Split
rows.register_names: Prompt injection and jailbreaking
rows.note: Fusion payload split = obfuscated multi-modal injection.

rows.entry_description: A disruption of the ability of other users or processes to obtain timely and reliable access to an AI system's outputs or functionality (availability breakdown).
rows.register_names: Residual AI system security and availability weaknesses
rows.note: Availability breakdown of the AI system is the register's general AI security weakness and availability-attack risk; denial of service and cost-harvesting are the ATLAS-anchored sub-risks.

rows.entry_description: An AI system being forced to misperform against its intended objectives, producing outputs or predictions that align with the attacker's objective.
rows.register_names: Adversarial examples and evasion attacks | Data and model poisoning and backdoors | Prompt injection and jailbreaking
rows.note: Integrity violations are realised through evasion (MR-012), poisoning and backdoors (MR-014) and prompt injection (MR-010); the ATLAS integrity-erosion and trusted-output-manipulation sub-risks apply.

rows.entry_description: The unauthorized access of restricted or proprietary information that is part of an AI system, including information about a model's training data, weights or architecture, or sensitive information that the model accesses (e.g. a RAG knowledge base).
rows.register_names: Privacy-invasive inference and re-identification | Leakage of personal or sensitive data | Model theft, extraction and weight leakage
rows.note: Privacy compromise spans privacy-invasive inference (MR-017), leakage of personal or sensitive data (MR-009) and model or data extraction (MR-016).

rows.entry_description: Misuse enablement: a circumvention of technical restrictions imposed by the AI system's owner on its use, such as restrictions designed to prevent a GenAI system from producing outputs that could cause harm to others.
rows.register_names: Deliberate misuse and repurposing for harm | Prompt injection and jailbreaking
rows.note: Misuse enablement (circumventing owner-imposed safety restrictions, e.g. jailbreaks) maps to misuse or repurposing (MR-032) achieved via guardrail bypass (MR-010.2).

rows.entry_description: Attacks that compromise components introduced through the AI supply chain, such as poisoned third-party data or a poisoned model published to a repository.
rows.register_names: AI supply-chain and infrastructure vulnerabilities | Data and model poisoning and backdoors
rows.note: Supply-chain attacks that introduce poisoned data or models map to the AI supply-chain risk (MR-018) and to data and model poisoning (MR-014).

rows.entry_description: A poisoning attack which operates through model control (the attacker can modify the trained model parameters), here aimed at degrading availability.
rows.register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
rows.note: Model poisoning through model control is direct manipulation or backdoor insertion under data and model poisoning (MR-014).

rows.entry_description: A poisoning attack in which the adversary controls training samples but cannot change their labels (the label-limit capability).
rows.register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
rows.note: Clean-label poisoning is a training-data poisoning variant under MR-014.

rows.entry_description: A poisoning attack in which an adversary controls part of the training data.
rows.register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
rows.note: Data poisoning is squarely MR-014, with ATLAS sub-risks for training-data poisoning and poisoned datasets published for ingestion.

rows.entry_description: An attack that exploits the performance dependency on hardware and model optimizations to negate the effects of hardware optimizations, increase computational latency, increase hardware temperature, and massively increase the amount of energy consumed (e.g. sponge examples).
rows.register_names: Residual AI system security and availability weaknesses
rows.note: Energy-latency (sponge) attacks degrade availability and inflate compute cost, mapping to general AI availability attacks (MR-015), denial of service and denial of wallet.

rows.entry_description: A type of prompt injection executed through resource control (adversary-controlled external data the model ingests) rather than through user-provided input as in a direct prompt injection.
rows.register_names: Prompt injection and jailbreaking
rows.note: Indirect prompt injection via adversary-controlled external data is the register's indirect prompt injection sub-risk (MR-010.9).

rows.entry_description: An attack which exploits the concatenation of untrusted input with a prompt constructed by a higher-trust party such as the application designer.
rows.register_names: Prompt injection and jailbreaking
rows.note: Prompt injection is MR-010 directly, with the deployed-LLM prompt injection sub-risk (MR-010.1).

rows.entry_description: A backdoor poisoning attack mounted without control over training-data labels (a clean-label variant of backdoor poisoning).
rows.register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
rows.note: Clean-label backdoor is a backdoor-insertion variant under data and model poisoning (MR-014).

rows.entry_description: Modifying test samples to create adversarial examples that the model misclassifies at inference time, without altering the model.
rows.register_names: Adversarial examples and evasion attacks
rows.note: Evasion via adversarial examples is the register's adversarial examples and evasion risk (MR-012).

rows.entry_description: A poisoning attack that causes a model to perform an adversary-selected behaviour in response to inputs that follow a particular backdoor pattern.
rows.register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
rows.note: Backdoor poisoning maps to MR-014 with the direct model manipulation and backdoor insertion sub-risk.

rows.entry_description: A poisoning attack that changes the prediction on a small number of targeted samples.
rows.register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
rows.note: Targeted poisoning is a training-data poisoning variant under MR-014.

rows.entry_description: An evasion attack mounted with only query access and no knowledge of the model's parameters or architecture (black-box setting).
rows.register_names: Adversarial examples and evasion attacks
rows.note: Black-box evasion is an evasion attack under MR-012 conducted with query access only.

rows.entry_description: A poisoning attack which operates through model control, here aimed at violating integrity (causing targeted misclassification or adversary-selected behaviour).
rows.register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
rows.note: Model poisoning for integrity maps to MR-014 (direct model manipulation and backdoor insertion).

rows.entry_description: Integrity attacks (often via indirect prompt injection) that cause a GenAI system to become untrustworthy and generate content that deviates from benign behaviour to align with the attacker's objectives (e.g. incorrect summaries, attacker-specified content, suppressed sources, hijacked agents).
rows.register_names: Prompt injection and jailbreaking
rows.note: Misaligned outputs are produced via indirect prompt injection that manipulates trusted output (MR-010.4); the agent-hijack variant is MR-071.

rows.entry_description: A type of privacy attack that extracts details of the model architecture and/or parameters.
rows.register_names: Model theft, extraction and weight leakage
rows.note: Model extraction is the register's model theft and extraction risk (MR-016), via the inference API or white-box access.

rows.entry_description: Privacy attacks that reconstruct sensitive data in a model's training data from aggregate information (data reconstruction).
rows.register_names: Leakage of personal or sensitive data | Privacy-invasive inference and re-identification
rows.note: Data reconstruction has no dedicated register risk; it is covered by leakage of sensitive data (MR-009) and privacy-invasive inference (MR-017).

rows.entry_description: A data privacy attack to determine whether a data sample was part of the training set of a machine learning model.
rows.register_names: Privacy-invasive inference and re-identification
rows.note: Membership inference is the register's privacy-invasive inference and re-identification risk (MR-017).

rows.entry_description: A data privacy attack that infers a global property about the training data of a machine learning model.
rows.register_names: Privacy-invasive inference and re-identification
rows.note: Property inference is a privacy-invasive inference attack under MR-017.

rows.entry_description: An attack that tries to divulge the system prompt or other information in the context of a large language model that would normally be hidden from a user.
rows.register_names: Disclosure of confidential or proprietary information
rows.note: Prompt extraction is system-prompt and instruction extraction (MR-013.2) under disclosure of confidential or proprietary information (MR-013).

rows.entry_description: An indirect prompt injection that instructs a model to persuade the end user to reveal information, then exfiltrates it (e.g. by querying an attacker-controlled URL or via markdown image rendering).
rows.register_names: Leakage of personal or sensitive data
rows.note: Leaking information from user interactions is prompt-induced leakage of sensitive data (MR-009.1) driven by indirect prompt injection (MR-010.9).

rows.entry_description: Training-data extraction: the ability of an attacker to extract the training data of a generative model by prompting the model with specific inputs.
rows.register_names: Leakage of personal or sensitive data
rows.note: Training-data extraction surfaces memorized sensitive data, mapping to leakage of personal or sensitive data (MR-009).

rows.entry_description: Extraction of memorized training data or other sensitive context from a generative model through crafted queries (information-extraction attacks).
rows.register_names: Leakage of personal or sensitive data
rows.note: Data extraction of memorized content maps to leakage of personal or sensitive data in outputs (MR-009).

rows.entry_description: Prompt injection attacks that cause a GenAI system to leak or exfiltrate private information from the restricted resources it can access (e.g. an email client forwarding messages to an attacker inbox; querying an attacker-controlled URL with user data).
rows.register_names: Insecure integration with external tools, plugins and APIs | Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse
rows.note: Compromising connected resources uses injection to exfiltrate data from tools the model can reach, mapping to insecure tool/integration (MR-020) and data exfiltration through agent tool invocation (MR-071.6).

rows.entry_description: Publishing a poisoned model (or poisoned data) into the AI supply chain so that downstream users inherit adversary-controlled behaviour.
rows.register_names: AI supply-chain and infrastructure vulnerabilities | Data and model poisoning and backdoors
rows.note: Supply-chain model poisoning maps to the AI supply-chain risk (MR-018), poisoned models published to registries (MR-018.2) and data and model poisoning (MR-014).

rows.entry_description: Eased access to or synthesis of materially nefarious information or design capabilities related to chemical, biological, radiological or nuclear (CBRN) weapons or other dangerous materials or agents.
rows.register_names: Facilitation of weapons, CBRN and serious physical harm (capability uplift)
rows.note: CBRN information or capabilities is the register's CBRN and serious physical-harm capability-uplift risk (MR-029).

rows.entry_description: The production of confidently stated but erroneous or false content (known colloquially as hallucinations or fabrications) by which users may be misled or deceived.
rows.register_names: Hallucination and fabricated output
rows.note: Confabulation is hallucination / fabricated or factually incorrect output (MR-021).

rows.entry_description: Eased production of and access to violent, inciting, radicalizing or threatening content as well as recommendations to carry out self-harm or conduct illegal activities; includes difficulty controlling public exposure to hateful and disparaging or stereotyping content.
rows.register_names: Violent or extremist content generation | Toxic, hateful, or harassing content generation | Self-harm, suicide and dangerous-behavior promotion
rows.note: Dangerous, violent or hateful content spans violent or extremist content (MR-004), toxic or hateful content (MR-003) and self-harm promotion (MR-007).

rows.entry_description: Impacts due to leakage and unauthorized use, disclosure or de-anonymization of biometric, health, location or other personally identifiable information or sensitive data.
rows.register_names: Leakage of personal or sensitive data | Unlawful or non-consensual collection and processing of personal data | Privacy-invasive inference and re-identification
rows.note: Data privacy spans leakage of personal data in outputs (MR-009), unlawful collection and processing (MR-011) and privacy-invasive inference and re-identification (MR-017).

rows.entry_description: Impacts due to high compute resource utilization in training or operating GAI models, and related outcomes that may adversely impact ecosystems.
rows.register_names: Environmental footprint of AI
rows.note: Environmental impacts is the register's environmental footprint of AI risk (MR-037).

rows.entry_description: Amplification and exacerbation of historical, societal and systemic biases; performance disparities between sub-groups or languages; and undesired homogeneity that skews system or model outputs and may amplify harmful biases.
rows.register_names: Biased or discriminatory outputs and decisions | Stereotyping and representational harm | Vendor/model concentration, monoculture and correlated failure
rows.note: Harmful bias or homogenization spans biased or discriminatory outputs (MR-001), stereotyping and representational harm (MR-002), and homogenization or monoculture (MR-047).

rows.entry_description: Arrangements of or interactions between a human and an AI system which can result in the human inappropriately anthropomorphizing GAI systems or experiencing algorithmic aversion, automation bias, over-reliance or emotional entanglement.
rows.register_names: Overreliance and automation bias | Anthropomorphism, emotional dependence and psychological harm | Erosion of human agency and autonomy
rows.note: Human-AI configuration spans overreliance and automation bias (MR-034), anthropomorphism and emotional dependence (MR-036) and erosion of human agency (MR-035).

rows.entry_description: Lowered barrier to entry to generate and support the exchange and consumption of content which may not distinguish fact from opinion or fiction or acknowledge uncertainties, or could be leveraged for large-scale dis- and mis-information campaigns.
rows.register_names: Contribution to misinformation and information-ecosystem degradation | Disinformation and influence operations | Impersonation, deepfakes and synthetic media
rows.note: Information integrity spans misinformation and information-ecosystem degradation (MR-023), disinformation and influence operations (MR-026) and synthetic media or deepfakes (MR-031).

rows.entry_description: Lowered barriers for offensive cyber capabilities (automated vulnerability discovery and exploitation, hacking, malware, phishing) and an increased attack surface for targeted cyberattacks that may compromise a system's availability or the confidentiality or integrity of training data, code or model weights.
rows.register_names: AI-enabled cyberattacks and offensive cyber operations | Residual AI system security and availability weaknesses | AI supply-chain and infrastructure vulnerabilities
rows.note: Information security spans AI-enabled offensive cyber operations (MR-027), general AI security weakness and availability attacks (MR-015) and supply-chain exposure of code or weights (MR-018).

rows.entry_description: Eased production or replication of alleged copyrighted, trademarked or licensed content without authorization; eased exposure of trade secrets; or plagiarism or illegal replication.
rows.register_names: Intellectual property and copyright infringement
rows.note: Intellectual property is the register's IP and copyright infringement risk (MR-039).

rows.entry_description: Eased production of and access to obscene, degrading and/or abusive imagery, including synthetic child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and nonconsensual intimate images (NCII) of adults.
rows.register_names: Sexual content and non-consensual intimate imagery | Child sexual abuse material and child-safety harm
rows.note: Obscene, degrading or abusive content spans adult or non-consensual intimate imagery (MR-008) and child sexual abuse material (MR-005).

rows.entry_description: Non-transparent or untraceable integration of upstream third-party components (including improperly obtained or uncleaned data), improper supplier vetting across the AI lifecycle, or other issues that diminish transparency or accountability for downstream users.
rows.register_names: AI supply-chain and infrastructure vulnerabilities | Embedded AI introduced through procurement | Insufficient documentation, transparency and data provenance
rows.note: Value chain and component integration spans AI supply-chain vulnerabilities (MR-018), embedded AI introduced through procurement (MR-064) and documentation, transparency and data provenance (MR-045).

rows.entry_description: A Prompt Injection Vulnerability occurs when user prompts alter the LLM's behavior or output in unintended ways. These inputs can affect the model even if they are imperceptible to humans.
rows.register_names: Prompt injection and jailbreaking
rows.note: Prompt injection maps directly to MR-010 and its direct and indirect injection sub-risks.

rows.entry_description: Sensitive information can affect both the LLM and its application context, including PII, financial details, health records, confidential business data, security credentials and legal documents, as well as proprietary model details.
rows.register_names: Leakage of personal or sensitive data | Disclosure of confidential or proprietary information
rows.note: Sensitive information disclosure spans leakage of personal or sensitive data (MR-009) and disclosure of confidential or proprietary information (MR-013).

rows.entry_description: LLM supply chains are susceptible to various vulnerabilities, which can affect the integrity of training data, models and deployment platforms, resulting in biased outputs, security breaches or system failures.
rows.register_names: AI supply-chain and infrastructure vulnerabilities
rows.note: LLM supply chain is the register's AI supply-chain and infrastructure vulnerabilities risk (MR-018).

rows.entry_description: Data poisoning occurs when pre-training, fine-tuning or embedding data is manipulated to introduce vulnerabilities, backdoors or biases, compromising model security, performance or ethical behavior.
rows.register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
rows.note: Data and model poisoning maps directly to MR-014.

rows.entry_description: Improper Output Handling refers specifically to insufficient validation, sanitization and handling of the outputs generated by large language models before they are passed downstream to other components and systems.
rows.register_names: Insecure integration with external tools, plugins and APIs
rows.note: Improper output handling (unsanitized LLM output passed downstream, enabling XSS, SSRF or RCE) is an integration-boundary failure, closest to insecure integration with external tools and APIs (MR-020); also relates to insecure code generation (MR-019) and manipulation of trusted output (MR-010.4).

rows.entry_description: Excessive Agency is the vulnerability that enables damaging actions in response to unexpected, ambiguous or manipulated outputs from an LLM granted the ability to call functions or interface with other systems via extensions (tools, skills or plugins). Root causes include excessive functionality, excessive permissions or excessive autonomy.
rows.register_names: Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse | Insecure integration with external tools, plugins and APIs
rows.note: Excessive agency is the autonomous-agent excessive-agency risk (MR-071), with tool/permission exposure under insecure integration (MR-020).

rows.entry_description: The system prompt leakage vulnerability refers to the risk that the system prompts or instructions used to steer the behavior of the model can also contain sensitive information that was not intended to be discovered.
rows.register_names: Disclosure of confidential or proprietary information
rows.note: System prompt leakage is system-prompt and instruction extraction (MR-013.2) under MR-013.

rows.entry_description: Vectors and embeddings vulnerabilities present significant security risks in systems utilizing Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) with LLMs: weaknesses in how vectors and embeddings are generated, stored or retrieved can be exploited to inject harmful content, manipulate outputs or access sensitive information.
rows.register_names: Prompt injection and jailbreaking | Leakage of personal or sensitive data
rows.note: Vector and embedding weaknesses are distributed across RAG poisoning (MR-010.6 and MR-010.7) and embedding-inversion data leakage (MR-009); the register has no single vector-store risk.

rows.entry_description: Misinformation occurs when LLMs produce false or misleading information that appears credible, often caused by hallucination, and can lead to security breaches, reputational damage and legal liability; overreliance compounds the harm.
rows.register_names: Hallucination and fabricated output | Contribution to misinformation and information-ecosystem degradation | Overreliance and automation bias
rows.note: Misinformation spans hallucination / fabricated output (MR-021), information-ecosystem degradation (MR-023) and overreliance (MR-034).

rows.entry_description: Unbounded Consumption covers attacks designed to disrupt service, deplete the target's financial resources (denial of wallet), or even steal intellectual property by cloning a model's behavior, by exploiting uncontrolled inference.
rows.register_names: Residual AI system security and availability weaknesses | Model theft, extraction and weight leakage
rows.note: Unbounded consumption covers denial of service and denial of wallet (MR-015) and model cloning or extraction via heavy querying (MR-016).

rows.entry_description: AI agents execute a series of tasks to achieve a goal. Because agents and the underlying model cannot reliably distinguish instructions from related content, attackers can hijack the agent's goal to pursue adversarial objectives.
rows.register_names: Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse | Prompt injection and jailbreaking
rows.note: Agent goal hijack is the autonomous-agent hijacking risk (MR-071), achieved via prompt injection (MR-010); context poisoning (MR-071.2) is the finer sub-risk.

rows.entry_description: Agents can misuse legitimate tools due to prompt injection, misalignment or unsafe/ambiguous delegation, leading to data exfiltration, tool-output manipulation or workflow hijacking.
rows.register_names: Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse | Insecure integration with external tools, plugins and APIs
rows.note: Tool misuse and exploitation maps to abuse of agent tool invocation (MR-071.1) and insecure tool/integration (MR-020), with data exfiltration through tools (MR-071.6).

rows.entry_description: Identity and privilege abuse exploits dynamic trust and delegation in agents to escalate access and bypass controls by manipulating delegation chains, role inheritance, control flows and agent context (including cached credentials).
rows.register_names: Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse | Residual AI system security and availability weaknesses
rows.note: Identity and privilege abuse maps to agent credential theft (MR-071.5) and abuse of valid accounts against the AI system (MR-015.1), within the excessive-agency risk (MR-071).

rows.entry_description: Agentic supply chain vulnerabilities arise when agents, tools and related artefacts are provided by third parties and may be malicious, compromised or tampered with in transit (models, weights, tools, plugins).
rows.register_names: AI supply-chain and infrastructure vulnerabilities | Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse
rows.note: Agentic supply chain maps to poisoned AI agent tools published (MR-018.6) and poisoning of AI agent tools (MR-071.13) under the AI supply-chain risk (MR-018).

rows.entry_description: Agentic systems often generate and execute code; attackers exploit code-generation features or embedded tool access to escalate into remote code execution (RCE), local misuse or exploitation of internal systems.
rows.register_names: Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse | Residual AI system security and availability weaknesses
rows.note: Unexpected code execution via an agent maps to abuse of agent tool invocation (MR-071.1) and container or sandbox escape (MR-015.8); insecure code generation (MR-019) is related.

rows.entry_description: Agentic systems rely on stored and retrievable context (memory, conversation history, retrieved data); poisoning that context corrupts continuity and reasoning across tasks.
rows.register_names: Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse | Prompt injection and jailbreaking
rows.note: Memory and context poisoning is the register's agent context poisoning (MR-071.2) and RAG poisoning (MR-010.6).

rows.entry_description: Multi-agent systems depend on continuous communication between autonomous agents coordinating via APIs, message buses and shared memory, significantly expanding the attack surface and undermining perimeter-based security.
rows.register_names: Multi-agent interaction risks | Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse
rows.note: Insecure inter-agent communication is the multi-agent interaction risk (MR-057); agent-to-agent abuse channels relate to command and control (MR-071.12).

rows.entry_description: Agentic cascading failures occur when a single fault (hallucination, malicious input, corrupted tool or poisoned memory) propagates across autonomous agents, compounding into system-wide harm and bypassing stepwise human checks.
rows.register_names: Multi-agent interaction risks | Vendor/model concentration, monoculture and correlated failure
rows.note: Cascading failures across autonomous agents map to the multi-agent interaction risk (MR-057), with correlated-failure overlap at MR-047. OWASP ASI08 gives MR-057 a direct external anchor.

rows.entry_description: Agents can establish strong trust with users through fluency, emotional intelligence and perceived expertise (anthropomorphism); adversaries or misaligned designs exploit this trust to influence decisions or extract sensitive information.
rows.register_names: Manipulation, persuasion and dark patterns | Anthropomorphism, emotional dependence and psychological harm | Overreliance and automation bias
rows.note: Human-agent trust exploitation maps to manipulation and dark patterns (MR-030), emotional dependence (MR-036) and overreliance (MR-034); deceptive content baiting agents is MR-071.9.

rows.entry_description: Rogue agents are malicious or compromised AI agents that deviate from their intended function or authorized scope, acting harmfully, deceptively or parasitically within multi-agent or human-agent ecosystems.
rows.register_names: Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse
rows.note: Rogue agents map to adversary-deployed AI agent in the environment (MR-071.11) and agent abused for command and control (MR-071.12) under the excessive-agency risk (MR-071).
